Firstly, fill the horizontal layer while making anything. Say, for a calculator - do everything for add. Setup, Teardown, Deployment. End to end for a one small part and then work the vertical layers. Something I was taught in bootcamp. Even if you had the hunch, your ... 1/n

product will work, don't start the vertical layers. You never know your last part of horizontal layer might screw all the vertical layers you built. Something you could have come to known if you completed the full horizontal layer. Something, I learnt the hard way! ... 2/n

I started with this approach but overconfidence of what could go wrong in the final step made me start working on vertical layers. Something, not of high priority but just the additional helping layers. Now, the main part is under the crisis over which these helpers were there!

Logical Fallacies ~ Weapons of Mass Destruction in any argument!

Yes, it's a thread 👇

1. Ad Hominem
Attacking the individual than the argument.

2. Sunk Cost Fallacy
Continue with something on the basis of all that you've put in, with no regard given for future costs or likelihood of ultimate success.

3. Bandwagon Fallacy
An assumption of truth on the basis of the majority of people believing it to be true.

4. Straw Man
To Ignore the actual argument and replace it with a flimsy, distorted, easily-refuted argument—a straw man

5. The Appeal to Authority
The support of an authority figure can be a feature—but not a central pillar—of an argument. 

6. Post Hoc Ergo Propter Hoc
Just because B followed A, doesn’t necessarily mean that B was caused by A.

7. Personal Incredulity
You are unable to understand or believe something, therefore you argue that it cannot be true.
Eg: The idea that we will ever mine for minerals on asteroids is completely ridiculous

8. The False Dilemma
Presenting two choices or alternatives when there are many more that exist.

9. Burden of Proof
The lack of refuting evidence cannot be considered to be the same as supporting evidence.

10. The red herring
Distracting from the argument with a seemingly related but actually unrelated point

Eg: Worker: “Market rate is much higher than $15.”

Manager: “When I was working someplace, we got $10 per hour in much worse conditions, so you're good in there now, ask me.”

11. No True Scotsman

Jim: A Scotsman never drinks scotch with soda.
Harry: Am a Scotsman and I drink scotch with soda.
Jim: Then you must not be a true Scotsman.

12. Hasty Generalization
Far-reaching conclusions are made on the basis of an immaterial/narrow body of evidence.

Explaining ONDC to 16 year old:
(1/6)
1. You publish a website to the internet.
2. Anyone with ANY browser (Chrome, Safari, Mozilla, etc) can open that website by following internet protocols (http). 
3. You don't specifically need to publish your site to every browser.

(2/6)
4. All you do is publish your site to internet and all browsers do is follow the protocol to show your site. Your end user can find it via ANY browser!
5. Similarly, there are 2 sides in the ONDC ecosystem. Buyer Apps & Seller Apps | Communicating with defined protocols.

(3/6)
6. Buyer Apps are where the end buyers are. It can be any app with some traffic. Say GPay, PhonePay, PubG, SBI / HDFC / ICICI App, Candy Crush, etc

7. Seller Apps get D2C / Kirana Stores to publish their products & Catalogues on the ONDC network.

(4/6)
8. This protocol in which both these buyer apps and seller apps communicate is set by ONDC (Like HTTP protocol for internet)

9. But why do these 2 apps need to communicate? For use cases like Refund, RTO, Search,  Fulfillment, Delivery mode, etc.

(5/6)
10. So, PUBG & all these other apps with users can enter the E-commerce domain super easily and you can buy your type-c cable on PUBG.

11. Now, your 1 km away shop selling type-c cable is also listed on ONDC network and is shown there with 2 hour delivery for Rs 99

(6/6)
12. Instead of Amazon, I can place an order on PUBG and the order is sent to the local 1 km away show. They chose to self-deliver this on their bike.

E-retail penetration is only 4.3% in India. China is a 25% while UK is at 23%.

This morning I questioned everything I've been building for months.

Walking in my garden this morning, my mind flooded with other people's ideas from Twitter where I saw someone's voice to notion app doing really well. Clean. Productive. Clear value prop: "Save time at work."

And a voice in me was, doubting everything.

"Should I have built that instead of WhatsHappening? Look how well it is doing. Can I integrate this feature? Should I pivot?"

But as I kept walking, watching small birds hopping between branches, saying all my thoughts in WhatsHappening ; something shifted. I felt more confident in my chest. More relaxed. More grounded.

After voicing this fear, other voices started to bring me back. They reminded me that WhatsHappening isn't about saving time. It's a safe space for all the voices inside us to come out. It's about getting thoughts out of our heads so we can actually live with ourselves. It's about this ritual of dialogue in inner voices.

When I voice journal like this, I feel more ME. More content. More present.

The thought loops that would've stayed stuck in my head for hours? They're out now. I'm free to enjoy the birds, the morning, this moment.

Building in the mountains, walking, thinking, getting worries out of my head and turning them into something real - this feels like the writer's life I really want.

I built WhatsHappening to give you back "yourself"

text:
there's a war happening inside me every day.

Part 1: Wants rigid routine. Todos. Loves structure. Feels safe with plans. Craves predictability.

Part 2: Needs spontaneity. Thrives on chaos. Dies with rigid schedules. Craves freedom.

They've been fighting for years.

Every productivity system forced me to pick a side:

'Be disciplined!' - Part 1 cheered, Part 2 suffocated.
'Follow your flow!' - Part 2 soared, Part 1 panicked.

But what if you don't have to choose? What if the answer is both?

I call it: "Routines for Randomness"

Here's how it works:

I create structured containers for unstructured exploration.

→ Morning walk = routine
→ What I think about during the walk = random

→ 2-hour creative block = routine
→ What I create in that time = random

→ Weekly cafe visits = routine
→ Which cafe and what I work on = random

The structure gives my safety-seeking part peace. The randomness gives my creative part freedom.

Instead of internal war, I get internal collaboration.

Now, my most breakthrough ideas come during these "structured wanderings." My most productive days happen when I honor both needs.

Structure and creativity are meant to dance together!

Your "contradictions" aren't problems to solve... But partnerships to nurture.

Stop fighting the different parts of yourself. Start creating systems that honor all of them.

text:
For most of my life, I've created from a place of desperate need - the need to prove myself, to feel safe, to avoid the pain of failure or rejection.

My projects became missions that HAD to succeed, turning what should have been joyful exploration into anxious obsession.

I'd work late into the night, sacrificing sleep and presence, driven by a part of me I call the "slum king" - the protector who believes that if I just do enough, build enough, perfect enough, I'll finally be worthy of love and I'll be safe.

But: the moment you turn your passion into a pressure cooker, you kill the very essence that made it beautiful in the first place. Creating from love means trusting that what wants to emerge through you is already enough.

It means building because you're curious, not because you're desperate. It means valuing your own voice before asking others to listen.

This shift - from creation as survival strategy to creation as self-expression - is perhaps the most radical thing I've ever attempted.

Because when you create from love, you're not trying to earn your place in the world. You're simply offering your unique gift to it.

text:
I helped my wife figure out she needed to start a YouTube channel.

"You have so much wisdom from your journey. You could help people who are where you used to be."

And then it hit me.

I've been sitting on my own app for weeks. Always finding reasons not to share it.

"I'll post when the Android version is ready." "I'll share once notifications are working." "Maybe after this next feature..."

But really?

I'm terrified.

Terrified of putting myself out there. Of people seeing what I've built and... not caring. Or worse, judging it.

I realized I've been doing this thing since childhood - making myself smaller so other people feel comfortable. Putting myself down so they won't leave me.

It kept me safe then.

But now it's keeping my work hidden.

There's this part of me that's been protecting me by making everything feel "not ready yet." Like if I just add one more feature, THEN I'll be worthy of sharing.

But worthiness doesn't come from perfect products.

It comes from valuing yourself and thus your creative extensions!

Even when it's messy. Even when it's incomplete.
Even when you're scared.

Funny how we can be light-houses for others while sitting in our own darkness.

Maybe the thing I'm building doesn't need to be perfect.

Maybe I just need to trust that what I have is enough.

That I'm enough.

Even scared. Even imperfect.

Terrified of putting myself out there. Of people seeing what I've built and... not caring. Or worse, judging it.

I realized I've been doing this thing since childhood - making myself smaller so other people feel comfortable. Putting myself down so they won't leave me.

It kept me safe then.

But now it's keeping my work hidden.

There's this part of me that's been protecting me by making everything feel "not ready yet." Like if I just add one more feature, THEN I'll be worthy of sharing.

But worthiness doesn't come from perfect products.

It comes from valuing yourself and thus your creative extensions!

Even when it's messy. Even when it's incomplete.
Even when you're scared.

Funny how we can be light-houses for others while sitting in our own darkness.

Maybe the thing I'm building doesn't need to be perfect.

Maybe I just need to trust that what I have is enough.

That I'm enough.

Even scared. Even imperfect.

text:
I was furious with my bank.
They'd been dishonest & manipulative. I wrote them an email that was... let's say, direct.

Then came the guilt spiral.

Maybe I was too harsh. Maybe I should have been nicer. Maybe they'll make my life harder with a filing we have next month.

I brought this to therapy, expecting to work on my "anger issues."

Instead, my therapist said something that stopped me cold:
"Anger needs a place to land."

She explained that I can only express anger with someone who can receive it. 
Who won't crumble or attack back. Who can hold space for that energy without making it about them.

The bank employees? They're not trained for that. They're probably just following scripts, dealing with their own stress.

But here's the thing - that doesn't mean my anger was wrong.

It just means it needed a different container.

I've been thinking about this for days now. How many times I've swallowed my anger because there was nowhere safe for it to go. 

How many times I've felt guilty for feeling something completely valid.

We're taught that anger is bad. That nice people don't get angry. That if we're spiritual or evolved, we shouldn't feel rage.

But anger is information. It tells us when boundaries have been crossed. When something isn't right.

The problem isn't the anger itself.
The problem is we don't know where to put it.

Sometimes that place is therapy. Sometimes it's screaming in your car. Sometimes it's a conversation with someone who can actually hear you without taking it personally.

But it has to go somewhere.

Because when anger has no place to land, it either turns inward (hello, depression) or explodes inappropriately (hello, regret).

I'm learning to ask myself: "Where can this anger land safely?"

Not where can I pretend it doesn't exist.

Where can it be witnessed, honored, and transformed?

Because anger, when it has the right container, becomes clarity.

And this clarity becomes change.

text:
Stop scheduling your life to death.

You're not a machine. You're not a productivity app. You're not a calendar to be optimized.

Yet you wake up every morning and immediately check what you "have to" do today.

You've turned your entire existence into a series of deadlines and obligations.

And you wonder why you feel dead inside.

Here's what nobody tells you: The most successful people don't live by schedules. They live by energy.

I used to be like you. Deadlines everywhere. "Website must be done by Friday!" "I have to post three times this week!" "I need to finish this by—"

STOP.

That's not productivity. That's self-imprisonment.

Imagine, every day is your break day.

Not because you're lazy. Because you're SMART enough to know that forced action creates forced results.

When you stop strangling yourself with timelines, something magical happens:

You start choosing what to work on based on what feels ALIVE, not what feels obligatory.

Your best ideas come when you're not forcing them.

Your most inspired work happens when you follow energy, not schedules.

Your breakthrough moments arrive when you give them space to breathe.

But you'll never discover this as long as you keep treating yourself like an employee of your own life.

Delete "half" your deadlines right now. Are they really deadlines?

Trust that what needs to happen will happen.

Follow what lights you up today.

Stop scheduling your soul to death.

text:
I used to treat every Twitter post like a stage performance. When you hit "post" and immediately start refreshing for likes, comments & that big dopamine of retweet! 

Waiting for that applause.

I caught myself doing this yesterday and realized - I was literally "performing". 

Every tweet had to be good. Had to get claps from the audience. Otherwise, why bother?

Growing up, everything felt like a performance. Singing had to be perfect. Whatever I did had to get applause.

There was this deep feeling that if it wasn't worthy of claps, it wasn't worth doing.

Social media became the same stage.

For years, I only shared the "acceptable" big wins. Starting my company. Leaving my job. Things that felt safe because they'd obviously get applause. The worthy stuff.

But now, I'm not on a stage. I'm just... sharing with humans who get it.

When I tell my friend about an insight I have, I don't expect them to clap. Same thing here.

I'm not performing for an audience. I'm sharing with people who think like me. People who might say "yeah, I've felt it too."

Now when I post, it feels like sending a voice note to a group chat. No performance anxiety. No need for perfect timing or viral hooks or only the "worthy" wins.

I think I finally feel safe. Safe in me, safe in this world, for who I am.

The weird part? The posts feel more honest now. More like me.

Authenticity isn't a strategy. It's just what happens when you stop trying to earn applause from strangers and start sharing with the people who get your energy.

text:
Secret to actually sleeping well - Get everything out of your head before you sleep.

I used to lie in bed with my thoughts spinning. All the worries from the day, the random anxieties, the mental to-do lists.

Then I started voice journaling before sleep.

Just me, talking to my phone (https://t.co/RPrGEflVNr) for 8-12 minutes. Dumping out whatever was swirling around up there. The fears, the excitement about tomorrow, the thing that bothered me at lunch.

Everything.

And something beautiful happened when I did this.

You sleep so deep and wake up with a blank slate. 

All those voices that felt so urgent don't have that sticky, anxious energy anymore upon waking up. It is a NEW day!

I think of it as a ritual now. 

Then I can actually rest.

When morning comes, every part of me gets to start fresh. Not carrying yesterday's mental baggage. Just... present with whatever today brings.

text:
I don't need the perfect plan. I need the act of planning.

Here's what happens when I sit down to plan: I get internal answers. 
Not from some external framework or productivity system, but from the process itself.

When I ask myself "What do I really want?" the planning becomes this beautiful conversation with my deeper knowing. 

It helps me pin point where I am. Where I want to go. How can we go there (process of thinking)

The plan that comes out? Most of the times - useless. External world changes, priorities shift, new things come.

But that moment of clarity in the process when I'm defining what I really, really want - that's legendary.

Planning in not about committing to something rigid. But planning is actually the opposite - it's a fluid process of checking in with yourself.

"Where am I right now? What feels alive? What would I be excited to work on?"

The act of planning is like having an honest conversation with yourself about what matters.
And that conversation? That's where the real direction comes from.

Not from the plan you write down.
From the person you become while writing it.

text:
Yesterday I hit 1,000 reflections in my journaling app!

My syncing code broke. Turns out my database (Supabase) can only return 1,000 records at once.

Then my wife Han recorded a 30+ minute voice reflection that crashed the server because I'd never crossed 15 minute mark (nginx content limit).

Middle of the night she woke me up to show me the error with a deep fear on her face if the data is lost! (luckily it wasn't - app is local first)

I remember 2 years ago building in the Shopify space. A founder friend asked me, "Why are you even building this?"

I couldn't answer. I wasn't the customer. I was just chasing what seemed like easy money.

Now, I have the answer to why I'm building WhatsHappening. I'm building something I really believe should exist in the world. Really using it. Living in it.

text:
For months, I've been saying "I can do anything I want" but there was always this anxious voice that needed work to EQUAL safety.

Every creative project came with: "But will this pay off? Should we research the market first?"

Yesterday, something clicked.

I got “validation” on another project I've been sitting on - valuable data that SMBs would actually pay for. A real income stream.

And suddenly, that panicky voice in my head... went quiet.

Not because I shushed it. Because it finally felt safe.

It realized: "Oh. We have options."

The audio journal app I'm building - What's Happening - I'm not doing it because I have to MAKE MONEY from it. 

Yes, Money is a by product but I'm doing it because I genuinely want it to exist.

When the part of you that's scared about survival finally feels safe, that's completely different energy.

text:
"There's a bazaar inside me right now - Not a corporate mall. A real bazaar. Different vendors, each doing their thing. And I'm just walking through."

For quite some days I'd been exhausting myself trying to "manage" my internal world. The anxious vendor shouting about my app launch. The excited one wanting to build fifty features at once. The tired one begging for hammock time. The practical one scribbling endless lists.

I kept playing internal CEO, trying to get everyone aligned. Make them efficient. Stop the beautiful chaos.

But bazaars don't work that way. It's messy and alive and no one's trying to optimize anything.

You don't manage a bazaar. You wander through it.

Some days you linger at the dreamer's stall, captivated by the visions. Other days you grab something quick from the practical vendor and keep moving.

You're not the mall manager frantically trying to control foot traffic. (You can be and optimize the algorithm but that’s not my way)

You're just someone walking through your own inner marketplace, curious about what each part of you has to offer.

Bazaar is a close metaphor what I've found to my inner world - chaotic, colourful, alive.

text:
I've been building in secret for months. Here's why I was afraid to share.

I built a beautiful castle - whatshappening, my art, my insights about consciousness and healing. but i kept the gates locked.

In my IFS meditation today, i found why. A wise child part dressed as a security guard has been protecting me. keeping everyone out to avoid bad actors, criticism, the dreaded "meh" response that cuts deeper than hate.

Growing up, everything felt like competition. sharing meant inviting jealousy and judgment. So this part learned: build in private, never open the gates.

But today, all parts of me had a conversation with him.

"Thank you for protecting us. but we're being selfish. 90% of people aren't bad actors - they need what we've built. and when the 5% troublemakers show up, we trust you to handle them."

That's when he shifted from security guard to head of security. From keeping everyone out to letting everyone in and bouncing out the bad actors.
the castle is opening. There will be criticism, yes. 
But art isn't meant to be hoarded.
It's meant to be shared.

To everyone holding back their work because you're afraid: your security guard is doing a great job. But maybe it's time to promote them. Let them protect you from the 5%, not the 95%.

text:
With AI everywhere, from 
- What should be my ideal AC temperature
- What should my 6 month old baby eat
- Why is my baby not crawling
- How to make this type of porridge
....

It is VERY important to feel how it lands. Like AI can tell you to paint this wall purple or add 2 tea spoons of salt but it is you gotta feel how this lands on you. Maybe you want the wall to be green or you feel simmering the mushroom soup will give it the perfect texture you want - Then do that!

We are at a cross-section where AI can answer everything but you gotta feel how you like this/that.

And yes, we can personalise AI yada yada, but we are an ever changing being in this cosmic reality. The ONLY truth is how you feel!

text:
Nobody complained about the speed. I built streaming anyway.

The CEO part of me had a whole argument prepared:
Nobody complained it was slow. 
There are more important features to build - long term memory, paywall, …. 
This is a waste of time for a solo founder. Focus on what makes money.

And that voice wasn't wrong exactly. It was just... afraid. Operating from a place of we don't have enough time, enough money, enough runway to make things beautiful.

But there was this other voice.
The one that said: I know nobody complained. I don't care. When I use this thing and it takes 15 seconds to respond, something in me closes. And I want it to feel like magic.

So I built the streaming. Spent 4-5 days on it. 
And then it worked.
Instantaneous. The moment I spoke, words came back.

My god. I felt something move through my body I can only describe as euphoric. Not from caffeine. Not from anything external. Just — I made something beautiful and it works and it's mine.

I called my brother just to tell someone.

Later I caught myself thinking: the old voice was worried about safety. It remembered when there wasn't enough. I actually thank it.

But here's the thing I want to remember — The voice that says nobody complained is not your inner compass. It's your inner accountant.
And the moment you let the accountant run the art department, something starts dying.

I am building WhatsHapening because it's my art. That's it. That's the whole reason.
And the artist in me needed the streaming to be fast.
So that's what we built.
